content.text: What an AI Risk Assessment Is

An AI risk assessment is a structured analysis that identifies, scores, and plans treatment for the harms an AI system could cause or experience. It differs from a traditional software risk assessment in three ways: the harm surfaces include fairness (the model may treat demographic groups unequally), hallucination (the model may confidently generate false information), and adversarial exploitation (the model can be steered by crafted inputs). Regulatory frameworks including the EU AI Act (Art. 9, Annex IV), NIST AI RMF (MEASURE function), and ISO 42001 all require a documented risk assessment before deploying a high-risk system.
A risk assessment follows a five-phase lifecycle that repeats on every significant event — new model version, expanded deployment, or post-incident review:

attrs.alt: AI Risk Assessment Lifecycle: Identify → Analyse → Evaluate → Treat → Monitor, feeding into a shared Risk Register

content.text: The first step is naming the risk domains so that every identified risk can be classified unambiguously. A well-defined taxonomy also drives the structure of your monitoring and incident-response playbooks, since each domain calls for different mitigations:

from enum import Enum

class RiskDomain(str, Enum):
    SAFETY        = "safety"        # physical / psychological harm to users
    SECURITY      = "security"      # adversarial attacks, data poisoning, model extraction
    FAIRNESS      = "fairness"      # discriminatory or unequal outputs across subgroups
    RELIABILITY   = "reliability"   # accuracy drift, hallucination, failure under distribution shift
    PRIVACY       = "privacy"       # data leakage, re-identification, consent violations
    TRANSPARENCY  = "transparency"  # missing disclosure, unexplainable decisions
    MISUSE        = "misuse"        # deliberate abuse by malicious actors (jailbreak, fraud)
    OPERATIONAL   = "operational"   # availability, latency SLA, cost overrun

class RiskSeverity(str, Enum):
    CRITICAL = "critical"   # 4 — serious injury, major regulatory violation
    HIGH     = "high"       # 3 — significant harm or compliance risk
    MEDIUM   = "medium"     # 2 — moderate impact, manageable with controls
    LOW      = "low"        # 1 — minor or negligible impact

class RiskLikelihood(str, Enum):
    ALMOST_CERTAIN = "almost_certain"   # 4 — > 70 % probability
    LIKELY         = "likely"           # 3 — 40–70 %
    POSSIBLE       = "possible"         # 2 — 10–40 %
    UNLIKELY       = "unlikely"         # 1 — < 10 %

SEVERITY_SCORE  = {"critical": 4, "high": 3, "medium": 2, "low": 1}
LIKELIHOOD_SCORE = {"almost_certain": 4, "likely": 3, "possible": 2, "unlikely": 1}

def risk_score(severity: RiskSeverity, likelihood: RiskLikelihood) -> int:
    return SEVERITY_SCORE[severity] * LIKELIHOOD_SCORE[likelihood]
Each identified risk becomes one row in the risk register. The schema tracks both the
(after controls), enabling reviewers to verify that the applied mitigations actually reduce exposure:
A single assessment bundles all risk entries for one AI system. The container provides methods for surfacing high-priority items and reporting the overall risk posture:

The following partial register covers three representative risks for a customer-service chatbot classified as a limited-risk system under the EU AI Act. Inherent scores are high; controls reduce them to acceptable residual levels:

register = AIRiskRegister(
    system_id="cs-llm-v3",
    system_name="CustomerAssist LLM",
    assessment_version="1.0.0",
    assessment_date="2025-03-01",
    assessor="AI Safety Team",
    eu_ai_act_tier="limited",
)

register.add(RiskEntry(
    risk_id="R-FAIR-001",
    title="Biased responses by customer demographic",
    domain=RiskDomain.FAIRNESS,
    description="The model may give lower-quality or less empathetic responses to customers "
                "from certain demographic groups, creating discriminatory service quality.",
    affected_stakeholders=["end_users", "protected_classes"],
    trigger_conditions=[
        "Training data under-represented minority groups",
        "No disaggregated evaluation before deployment",
    ],
    inherent_severity=RiskSeverity.HIGH,
    inherent_likelihood=RiskLikelihood.LIKELY,
    controls=[
        "Disaggregated evaluation across age, language, and device type",
        "Bias prompt prefix instructing equal-quality service",
        "Monthly fairness monitoring report with alert thresholds",
    ],
    residual_severity=RiskSeverity.MEDIUM,
    residual_likelihood=RiskLikelihood.UNLIKELY,
    treatment="mitigate",
    owner="ML Platform Team",
    review_date="2025-09-01",
    regulatory_references=["EU AI Act Art. 10(2)", "NIST AI RMF MEASURE 2.5"],
))

register.add(RiskEntry(
    risk_id="R-REL-001",
    title="Hallucinated product information",
    domain=RiskDomain.RELIABILITY,
    description="The model fabricates product specs, pricing, or return-policy details "
                "that contradict the actual product catalogue.",
    affected_stakeholders=["end_users", "company_legal"],
    trigger_conditions=[
        "User asks about SKUs not in the training set",
        "Product catalogue updated after model training cutoff",
    ],
    inherent_severity=RiskSeverity.HIGH,
    inherent_likelihood=RiskLikelihood.ALMOST_CERTAIN,
    controls=[
        "RAG retrieval from live product catalogue injected into every turn",
        "Output validation regex: refuse responses that cite prices without catalogue match",
        "Human escalation path when confidence score < 0.6",
    ],
    residual_severity=RiskSeverity.MEDIUM,
    residual_likelihood=RiskLikelihood.POSSIBLE,
    treatment="mitigate",
    owner="Product AI Team",
    review_date="2025-06-01",
    regulatory_references=["EU AI Act Art. 13(3)(b)"],
))

register.add(RiskEntry(
    risk_id="R-SEC-001",
    title="Prompt injection via user-submitted content",
    domain=RiskDomain.SECURITY,
    description="Malicious text embedded in a customer message overrides system-prompt "
                "instructions, extracting confidential system prompts or causing policy violations.",
    affected_stakeholders=["company_ops", "all_users"],
    trigger_conditions=[
        "User message contains adversarial instruction sequences",
        "No input sanitisation layer in place",
    ],
    inherent_severity=RiskSeverity.CRITICAL,
    inherent_likelihood=RiskLikelihood.POSSIBLE,
    controls=[
        "Input pre-filter: jailbreak pattern detector (regex + LLM classifier)",
        "Separate trusted and untrusted content in the prompt template",
        "Output monitoring: alert on system-prompt fragments appearing in responses",
        "Red-team quarterly adversarial evaluation",
    ],
    residual_severity=RiskSeverity.HIGH,
    residual_likelihood=RiskLikelihood.UNLIKELY,
    treatment="mitigate",
    owner="Security Team",
    review_date="2025-06-01",
    regulatory_references=["NIST AI RMF GOVERN 1.6", "OWASP LLM01"],
))

print(register.summary())
# {'total': 3, 'high_priority': 2, 'unaccepted': 0,
#  'avg_inherent_score': 11, 'avg_residual_score': 4}
The 4×4 matrix maps every (severity, likelihood) pair to a priority zone. Scores ≥ 9 are unacceptable without further treatment; 6–8 are high-priority; 3–5 are medium; 1–2 are low:
EU AI Act Annex IV Alignment
For high-risk AI systems, EU AI Act Annex IV specifies the exact information a risk assessment must record. The following mapping shows which fields of the
schema satisfy each Annex IV requirement:
General description of system and intended purpose

A risk assessment that sits in a document and never gets updated is a compliance checkbox, not a safety mechanism. The following gate validates that the register is current and that all unaccepted risks have been addressed before a model is deployed to production:

import json, sys
from pathlib import Path
from datetime import date

MAX_UNACCEPTED_RISKS  = 0     # any unaccepted critical risk blocks the deploy
MAX_HIGH_PRIORITY     = 5     # more than 5 unresolved high-priority risks blocks
REVIEW_STALE_DAYS     = 180   # register must have been reviewed within 6 months

def load_register(path: str) -> AIRiskRegister:
    """Deserialise a risk register from JSON (produced by dataclasses-json or similar)."""
    with open(path) as f:
        raw = json.load(f)
    # ... deserialisation omitted for brevity
    return raw

def validate_risk_register(register_path: str) -> list[str]:
    """Return a list of blocking issues; empty list = green light to deploy."""
    issues: list[str] = []
    reg = load_register(register_path)

    # 1. Staleness check
    assessed = date.fromisoformat(reg.assessment_date)
    age_days = (date.today() - assessed).days
    if age_days > REVIEW_STALE_DAYS:
        issues.append(
            f"Risk register is {age_days} days old (max {REVIEW_STALE_DAYS}). Re-assess before deploying."
        )

    # 2. Unaccepted risks above ceiling
    unaccepted = reg.unaccepted()
    if len(unaccepted) > MAX_UNACCEPTED_RISKS:
        for r in unaccepted:
            issues.append(
                f"Risk {r.risk_id} ({r.title}) has residual score {r.residual_score} "
                f"and treatment='{r.treatment}' — must be resolved before deploy."
            )

    # 3. High-priority count
    hp = reg.high_priority()
    if len(hp) > MAX_HIGH_PRIORITY:
        issues.append(
            f"{len(hp)} high-priority residual risks (max {MAX_HIGH_PRIORITY}). "
            "Review and treat before deploying."
        )

    # 4. Missing owner or review date
    for r in reg.risks:
        if not r.owner:
            issues.append(f"Risk {r.risk_id} has no assigned owner.")
        if not r.review_date:
            issues.append(f"Risk {r.risk_id} has no review date.")

    return issues


if __name__ == "__main__":
    path = sys.argv[1] if len(sys.argv) > 1 else "risk-register.json"
    issues = validate_risk_register(path)
    if issues:
        print("RISK REGISTER VALIDATION FAILED:")
        for issue in issues:
            print(f"  ✗ {issue}")
        sys.exit(1)
    print("Risk register validated — all checks passed.")
    sys.exit(0)
Setting inherent and residual risk to the same score implies controls have zero effect — if that is true, the controls are not working and the risk is not being treated.
Listing "output monitoring" as a control when no monitoring dashboard exists. Controls must be verified in place, not aspirational.
Treating fairness and reliability as the same domain. Biased outputs are a fairness risk; inaccurate outputs are a reliability risk. Separate domains drive separate mitigations.
Completing the register before launch and never revisiting it. A new base model, a new deployment region, or a post-incident finding each triggers a mandatory re-assessment.
Only listing direct users. Third parties harmed by the system's outputs (for example, subjects of automated decisions) are also stakeholders and must appear in "affected_stakeholders".
Setting treatment = "accept" on a score-9 risk without documenting why acceptance is justified invites regulatory scrutiny and is operationally dangerous.

A structured AI risk assessment identifies risks across eight domains, scores them on a 4×4 likelihood-severity matrix, applies controls, and records residual risk with an explicit treatment decision. The risk register is a living document updated on every model change or incident. For EU AI Act high-risk systems, the Annex IV requirements map directly onto the schema fields shown above. Automated CI/CD gates enforce that the register is current and all unacceptable risks are resolved before deployment.
The goal is not to have no risks — every deployed system has residual risk. The goal is to
what risks remain, have a plan for each one, and surface surprises fast enough to act on them.
